name: All-over Composition
principle: No hierarchical focus — every part of canvas equally active; the eye wanders without rest or anchor
spatial_organization: Isotropic — no top/bottom/center priority; the painting is the same viewed from any rotation
color_distribution: Even distribution of all colors across full canvas surface; no color massing in zones
gesture_distribution: Overlapping layers of marks cover entire surface; density varies rhythmically but never approaches zero
viewer_experience: Immersive — viewer surrounded at proper scale, no single focal point; the entire painting is the focus; the eye never completes a scan but enters a continuous circulation; Pollock's large works approach environmental scale
scale_importance: Critical — must exceed human scale (200cm+) to achieve immersion; small-scale all-over compositions become claustrophobic rather than enveloping
emotional_effect: Overwhelming, enveloping, meditative through saturation; continuous activity paradoxically approaches stillness
design_challenges: Avoiding boredom through rhythm variation within uniformity; maintaining visual interest without hierarchy; preventing either excessive uniformity (tedium) or excessive variation (chaos)
balance_approach: Achieved through density and rhythm rather than positional balance; visual weight distributed through mark density and color temperature
animation_potential: Very High — can animate mark-by-mark construction or layer reveal; each new layer transforms the whole
art_historical_significance: First fully developed in Western art by Pollock, though Janet Sobel's earlier work was seen by Pollock; represents the most radical rejection of Renaissance compositional hierarchy; influenced Color Field's edge-to-edge approaches
technical_execution: Requires viewing the canvas from all sides; Pollock worked on floor to walk around; constant rotation of viewpoint; scale requires physical performance
ai_art_prompt_structure: all-over abstract composition, no focal point, equal visual weight throughout, [color palette], drip and pour marks, layered gesture, continuous field, edge to edge activity

name: Color Field
principle: Large areas of relatively unmodulated color dominate; color IS the content — no subordination of color to line or form
characteristics: edge relationships between color areas carry compositional tension
spatial_organization: Stratified or expansive — color areas define the space; shallow illusionistic depth created by warm/cool relationships; Rothko creates near-infinite optical depth through layering
color_distribution: Concentrated in large areas; edges and transitions between areas are the compositional events
gesture_distribution: Minimized or eliminated; texture present but sublimated to color experience
viewer_experience: Enveloping, meditative, emotionally direct; color bypasses intellectual analysis and communicates directly; at proper viewing distance the edges of rectangles blur and colors appear to breathe
scale_importance: Essential — Rothko specified paintings must be seen at close range to exceed the viewer's field of vision; 'I want to be very intimate and human. To paint a small picture is to place yourself outside your experience, to look upon an experience as a stereopticon view or with a reducing glass.'
emotional_effect: Contemplative, transcendent, sometimes oppressive (dark fields) or joyful (warm fields); direct emotional communication without narrative mediation
design_challenges: Avoiding decorativeness — color fields risk becoming mere decoration without the spiritual intent; maintaining tension in apparent simplicity; edge quality is everything
balance_approach: Proportional — the size ratios of color areas against each other and against the canvas create tension and release
animation_potential: High — layer-by-layer wash buildup can be animated; breathing edge animation possible
art_historical_significance: Established color as autonomous expressive medium independent of representation; opened path to Minimalism; philosophical influence on generations of painters
technical_execution: Multiple thin washes (Rothko used 10-15 layers); sized canvas to control absorbency; careful control of edge quality through masking or freehand application; studio conditions of humidity controlled
ai_art_prompt_structure: color field painting, large soft-edged rectangles of [colors], luminous washes, layered translucent color, meditative scale, soft glowing edges, Rothko-style depth

name: Gestural Field
principle: Bold gestural marks establish a dynamic field; gesture as primary compositional unit; the mark records the body's movement
characteristics: traces of revision and reworking visible
spatial_organization: Dynamic — gestural marks create kinetic spatial tension; implied movement; no stable depth plane; space activated by gesture direction
color_distribution: Often limited palette for maximum gestural clarity; color serves mark rather than field; in de Kooning and Mitchell, color is richer but always in service of gesture
gesture_distribution: Varied — major marks anchor composition with minor marks responding; rhythm and counterpoint between large and small
viewer_experience: Energetic, confrontational, physically engaging; viewer feels the body that made the marks; Kline's works suggest architectural scale — the body before vast structures
scale_importance: Very important — large scale makes body-scale marks visible as architectural elements; the mark must exceed human hand scale to register as gestural field rather than drawing
emotional_effect: Force, urgency, decisiveness; or in Mitchell and de Kooning — sensory abundance, complexity of feeling
design_challenges: Avoiding confusion — too many gestural marks create visual noise; the field must have rhythm and space between marks; the ground is part of the composition
balance_approach: Asymmetrical dynamic balance — contrasting directions, scales, and densities of gesture; no static symmetry; balance through counterforce
animation_potential: Very High — mark-by-mark revelation is the most natural animation; the stroke as temporal event
art_historical_significance: Kline's black-white gestural field was influential on 1960s minimal abstraction; de Kooning's gestural field influenced Neo-Expressionism; the 'action painting' definition (Rosenberg) was based on gestural field works
technical_execution: Full body involvement; standing at canvas; broad brush, palette knife, or squeegee; single decisive strokes alongside layered reworking
ai_art_prompt_structure: gestural abstract painting, bold brushstrokes, [color palette], dynamic marks, body-scale gesture, action painting energy, traces of movement, asymmetric balance

name: Vertical/Horizontal Bands (Zips)
principle: Vertical or horizontal linear elements ('zips') divide color fields and reveal rather than interrupt the field's continuity; the zip is not a boundary but a revelation of the field's existence
characteristics: single or multiple thin vertical or horizontal lines
lines contrast with fields in color, value, or width
compressed space between line and field edge
spatial_organization: Newman described the zip as creating a division of time and space simultaneously; the vertical zip mirrors human upright posture — Newman called it a correlate to the human scale; horizontal bands create horizon-line spatial layering
color_distribution: Zip contrasts with field — warm zip on cool field or vice versa; value contrast varies from subtle to stark
gesture_distribution: Minimal — Newman applied zips with masking tape edges and freehand fills; the 'gesture' is in the decision to divide
viewer_experience: Confrontational at scale — the viewer faces the zip as they face another person; the zip organizes the viewer's position in space; one zip creates reflection; multiple zips create rhythm and a walking-along experience
scale_importance: Critical — zips only function at human scale; the viewer must be able to look down the zip from close range and feel it extend above and below the field of vision
emotional_effect: Sublime, existential confrontation; the individual before the infinite; the heroic; solitude and dignity
design_challenges: Width, color, and position of zip relative to canvas edges must be precise; symmetrical zip placement is neutral, asymmetrical placement creates tension; multiple zips require careful rhythmic spacing
balance_approach: Achieved through proportion — the ratio of color fields created by the zip; Golden section and other proportional systems used implicitly
animation_potential: Medium — zip can be animated as light travelling down the canvas; color field can build gradually; zip's appearance as a 'reveal' moment is powerful
art_historical_significance: Newman's zips were incomprehensible to his contemporaries and only retrospectively recognized as anticipating Minimalism; the zip embeds Newman's philosophical program — the stripe is not the painting's subject, the painting is its own subject
technical_execution: Masking tape applied vertically/horizontally then removed while paint wet or dry; freehand application for soft-edged zips; painter's tape creates hard edge
ai_art_prompt_structure: color field with single vertical zip line, [field color] expanse, contrasting [zip color] narrow stripe, edge to edge vertical line, Newman-style zip painting, monumental scale, chromatic sublime

name: Asymmetrical Balance
principle: Visual equilibrium achieved without symmetry; differing elements on either side of implied center axis achieve balance through contrast of size, color, value, and texture
characteristics: large simple forms balanced against small complex forms
dark heavy areas balanced against light large areas
multiple small elements grouped to balance single large element
color weight used as balance mechanism
implied visual center of gravity off-axis
spatial_organization: Complex — multiple spatial decisions create a dynamic equilibrium; viewer's eye travels between balancing elements and finds rest in their relationship
color_distribution: Color weight used compositionally — small area of warm saturated color balances large cool muted area; chromatic hierarchy serves balance
gesture_distribution: Concentrated gestural activity in some areas, restraint in others; the composition breathes
viewer_experience: Satisfying resolution of tension; the eye accepts the balance as resolved even without symmetry; creates dynamic rest rather than static rest
scale_importance: Moderate — asymmetric balance functions across scales; effective at intimate and monumental scale
emotional_effect: Resolution, tension-and-release, psychological completeness; the satisfaction of achieved equilibrium
design_challenges: Avoiding decorative prettiness; balance must feel necessary rather than designed; Motherwell's Elegies achieve somber weight through asymmetric black oval placement
balance_approach: Multiple simultaneous balance mechanisms — value, color temperature, shape size, texture, chromatic intensity all contribute
animation_potential: Medium — balance can be shown building through successive element placement
art_historical_significance: Motherwell's literary-symbolic use of asymmetric balance links Abstract Expressionism to Symbolism; his Elegies demonstrate how asymmetric composition can carry philosophical weight
technical_execution: Constant stepping back to assess visual weight distribution; color and shape decisions interdependent; revision and scraping when balance fails
ai_art_prompt_structure: asymmetric abstract composition, [large dark form] balanced against [lighter open area], visual tension resolved through scale and color contrast, gestural abstraction, Motherwell-inspired balance

name: Edge-to-Edge
principle: Color runs fully to all four edges of the canvas with no internal hierarchy; the canvas edge crops an implied infinite field; the painting has no border — it is a window into a larger continuous surface
characteristics: canvas proportions actively chosen for their relationship to color
edge is a crop not a boundary
all areas of canvas activated to the margin
spatial_organization: Infinite implied extension — the canvas is a sample of a larger continuous field; spatial depth achieved through color rather than formal recession
color_distribution: Distribution determined by pour direction and gravity; color forms organic, irregular areas; no centralized massing
gesture_distribution: Non-gestural or minimally gestural; the event is the pour itself; Frankenthaler's poured stains are geological rather than calligraphic
viewer_experience: Liberating, open; the painting doesn't announce its edges as containment; peripheral vision catches the edges running off; creates the sensation of being inside the color field
scale_importance: High — edge-to-edge at small scale feels cramped; at large scale the implication of infinite extension becomes convincing
emotional_effect: Freedom, openness, the natural world's refusal of borders; color as environment rather than painting
design_challenges: Without internal hierarchy the painting's interest must come entirely from color relationships and the character of the stained forms; risk of decorativeness
balance_approach: Balance through color weight distribution — the colors' positions relative to each other and to the canvas center create informal balance
animation_potential: High — the pour event can be animated; color spreading from initial contact point outward is inherently temporal
art_historical_significance: Frankenthaler's edge-to-edge staining technique directly inspired Morris Louis and Kenneth Noland after their 1953 studio visit, launching the Washington Color School and the next generation of Color Field painting
technical_execution: Canvas laid flat; paint poured from one or multiple positions; canvas tilted to direct flow; result not fully known until dry; raw canvas absorbency determines edge quality
ai_art_prompt_structure: edge to edge color field, [colors] running to all canvas margins, poured stain technique, no internal borders, organic color forms reaching edges, atmospheric color, Frankenthaler soak stain

name: Compressed Center
principle: Multiple soft-edged rectangular forms stacked vertically, centered, with color fields above and below pressing inward; the forms appear to float or compress within the picture plane; characteristic of Rothko's mature 1950s-60s work
characteristics: floating rectangular forms centered on canvas
forms hover above and below center
ground color visible at all edges
soft, luminous edges on floating forms
vertical compression between forms and edges
color relationships between floating forms and ground create tension
spatial_organization: Shallow optical depth — warm color forms appear to advance against cool grounds and vice versa; the stacked forms create implied vertical axis without asserting it aggressively; the forms float in indeterminate space
color_distribution: Two to three dominant color masses; ground shows at edges; each color area precisely calibrated against others
gesture_distribution: Invisible — multiple thin washes create the color areas with no gestural incident; the painting's 'activity' is optical and atmospheric, not gestural
viewer_experience: At proper viewing distance (45-90cm from the canvas face), the soft edges of the rectangles begin to vibrate; the forms appear to breathe; the viewer feels the painting responding to their presence; Rothko wanted 'the relationship between the areas of color' to be the viewer's whole experience
scale_importance: Critical — Rothko specified exact viewing distances relative to canvas height; his large canvases must exceed the viewer's peripheral vision to create immersion
emotional_effect: Contemplative, transcendent, and depending on color: joyful, melancholic, tragic, or serene; Rothko's color choices across the career chart an emotional trajectory from optimism to despair
design_challenges: The proportions of each rectangle relative to the canvas and to each other are the entire compositional challenge; millimeter adjustments to placement change the entire emotional character
balance_approach: Centered vertical balance with chromatic asymmetry; the upper and lower forms need not be equal — their color difference creates asymmetric visual weight that must be calibrated
animation_potential: Very High — the wash-by-wash buildup of each form can be animated to reveal the painting's construction; the breathing edge can be animated as a subtle pulse
art_historical_significance: Rothko's centered floating rectangle composition is one of the most recognizable and influential compositional formulas in 20th-century art; it influenced Color Field painting, Minimalism, and continues to be referenced by contemporary painters
technical_execution: Canvas sized with rabbit-skin glue and one coat of dry pigment ground; multiple thin oil washes with large soft brush; masking tape sometimes used early in career, abandoned for freehand application later
ai_art_prompt_structure: Rothko-style floating rectangles, soft-edged [color] forms on [ground color] background, centered vertical stack, luminous edges, color field painting, meditative scale, translucent washes

name: Layered Depth
principle: Multiple visible layers of different media, marks, and color create a temporal and spatial depth; the painting reads as archaeological — different strata of mark-making coexist, none fully covering the others
characteristics: different mark types from different temporal moments visible
earlier layers visible through later marks
the painting has a history that is visible
spatial_organization: Palimpsest — deep visual archaeology; multiple spatial levels coexist without logical recession; near and far marks exist at the same visual plane; the depth is temporal rather than perspectival
color_distribution: Multiple color layers from different sessions visible simultaneously; color mixing through optical overlap; earlier warm colors visible through cool upper layers creating luminous depth
gesture_distribution: All gesture types present across different layers — washes below, scumbles above, scribbles and drawn marks on top; hierarchy of touch from broad to intimate
viewer_experience: Intimacy, discovery, the sense of witnessing accumulated thought; the viewer traces the painting's history; Twombly's layered works feel like documents of ancient events — scratched, marked, re-marked over time
scale_importance: Moderate — layered depth works across scales; intimate scale allows close examination of layer archaeology; large scale imposes the temporal accumulation on the body
emotional_effect: Contemplative, archaeological, literary; the emotion of time — memory, loss, the sediment of experience; particularly associated with Twombly's mythological reference in his layered works
design_challenges: Too many layers without restraint create muddy confusion; each layer must have its own character and leave the previous layer legible; the chronology must be partially readable
balance_approach: Balance emerges from accumulation — no single formal decision determines the whole; the composition reveals itself through the process
animation_potential: Extremely High — the layer-by-layer construction is the most natural and revealing animation of this compositional type; revealing the historical sequence of a Twombly canvas would be extraordinarily illuminating
art_historical_significance: Twombly's development of layered depth as a compositional strategy linked Abstract Expressionism to European Art Informel and opened the path to post-modern citation and layering strategies; his incorporation of written text into paint layers anticipates Neo-Expressionism and conceptual mark-making
technical_execution: Multiple sessions with drying time between; varied media in sequence (often: oil ground, then washes, then drawn marks, then crayon, then more paint); leaving accidents and pentimento visible; Twombly worked on canvases over years
ai_art_prompt_structure: layered abstract painting, multiple visible strata, [media types] accumulated in layers, archaeological depth, scribble over wash over ground, Twombly-style palimpsest, temporal accumulation, text fragments in paint
